event_blocks.summary: The video narrative unfolds with an aerial view of El Salvador's landscape, transitioning to Nayib Bukele's presidential victory in 2019 and his commitment to public safety, underscored by news montages and speeches. The focus shifts to CECOT, the 'Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' prison, introduced as a high-security facility built in response to a state of emergency and mass arrests, designed to house 64,000 gang members. A man in a red t-shirt gains access, undergoing rigorous security checks involving metal detectors, physical pat-downs, and ID verification, all filmed by a camera operator. The narrator emphasizes the prison's advanced technology, including facial recognition cameras and motion sensors, highlighting its purpose as a deterrent rather than a rehabilitation center. The man is escorted through corridors, past cells filled with tattooed inmates, and eventually to the armory, showcasing rows of rifles and security equipment, reinforcing the impossibility of escape due to the presence of 600 soldiers and 250 police officers, watchtowers, barbed wire, and 15-meter high concrete walls, all set against a backdrop of tense orchestral music and the constant hum of security systems.

El Salvador cityscape.description: An aerial view of a densely built city with greenery and a prominent volcano in the background, likely San Salvador, shown during a drive through the region, accompanied by car sounds and indistinct chatter from inside the van.

Tattooed inmates.description: Multiple inmates with extensive tattoos on their heads and bodies, wearing white uniforms and sitting on the floor in a prison corridor, surrounded by guards.

Soldiers in formation.description: Uniformed military personnel in helmets and tactical gear standing in rows during a public address by Bukele.

Prison exterior at night.description: A dimly lit prison yard with high walls, barbed wire, and a watchtower, shown during a vehicle approach, reinforcing the high-security environment, as voiceovers discuss the state of emergency.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, being escorted through the CECOT prison and observing the armory, gesturing and engaging in conversation with the guard, though his own speech is not heard in this segment.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in black uniforms, some wearing balaclavas, conducting security checks on the man, including scanning his ID, performing a physical pat-down, and escorting him through the facility.

Nayib Bukele.description: The President of El Salvador, shown in formal attire during a public address, speaking at a podium with the Salvadoran flag behind him, with Arabic subtitles translating his statements about guaranteeing the safety of the people, as an English male voiceover translates his words. He is also mentioned by a separate male voiceover discussing his strong stance against gangs.

News montage.description: A compilation of news clips showing armed soldiers in tactical gear during a state of emergency, with on-screen text from NBC News Now stating 'El Salvador records deadliest day in 30 years', and Arabic subtitles translating the context, accompanied by male voiceovers explaining that "El Salvador is under state of emergency" and that "a state of emergency was declared."

CECOT prison.description: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, signage reading 'ARMERÍA' and 'CECOT', featuring well-lit corridors and a weapons warehouse with organized racks of rifles. A narrator emphasizes its extreme security, stating it's guarded by 600 soldiers and 250 police officers, making escape impossible.

Camera operator.description: A person holding a professional camera with a stabilizer, filming the man in the red t-shirt as he goes through the security checkpoint inside the CECOT prison, visible in the background during the process.

Security table.description: A metal table with a bottle of hand sanitizer and a phone scanner, where the man hands over his belongings for inspection by the guard.

Metal detector.description: A large white and blue 'Intercept' metal detector with a red light strip, branded with 'Tek84 San Diego, CA USA', used to screen individuals entering the CECOT prison, which a narrator identifies as a 'high-tech device' for verifying both prisoners and staff.

Signage.description: Spanish signs including 'ARMERÍA' and 'CECOT' above the armory entrance, and 'PROHIBIDO FUMAR' (No Smoking) on the wall, indicating restricted zones and facility functions. On-screen text in Arabic and English also provides context, such as 'Now, we are going to the armory' and 'Here we are in the weapons warehouse,' which are echoed by the narrator's speech.

Guard in black uniform.description: A prison guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve, seen conversing with the Man in red t-shirt and walking ahead, but without direct dialogue in this segment.

Map of El Salvador.description: A digital map highlighting El Salvador, visually showing animated markers for gangs 'MS-13' and 'Barrio 18', which the narrator explicitly names as "two of the largest gangs in Latin America."

Watchtower and barbed wire.description: A close-up of a concrete watchtower with a guard and multiple layers of barbed wire fencing, visually reinforcing the prison's security, which the narrator details as part of the "19 watchtowers" and "barbed wire" reinforcing the walls.

Aerial night view of CECOT.description: An illuminated, grid-like aerial view of the CECOT prison complex at night, showcasing its extensive layout and high-security design with blue lighting.

Weapons warehouse.description: A secure, well-lit room within the CECOT prison containing organized racks of rifles and other restraints, marked by signage as 'ARMERÍA'. The narrator and on-screen text explicitly identify it as the 'weapons warehouse' and 'armory,' where various types of weaponry are stored.

Masked prison guard.description: A uniformed officer in black attire, wearing a cap and sunglasses, escorting the man through the prison and demonstrating firearms in the armory. While he visually speaks to the man, a distinct voice (possibly another guard or narrator) describes the weaponry, stating 'we have this other type of weaponry' and discussing 'announcing a war against the gang' in the background, aligning with the guard's demonstration.

Rifles.description: Multiple black rifles displayed on metal racks in the armory, with close-ups showing their design and handling by the guard, indicating standard issue weapons for the facility. A voice in the armory refers to them as 'this other type of weaponry' within the 'weapons warehouse'.

Handcuffs and chains.description: Metal handcuffs and chains laid out on a black table in the armory, suggesting additional restraints used within the prison system.

raw_segments: The video opens with an aerial view of a vast, rural landscape, transitioning into a prison corridor where a voice says 'Adelante'. A man in a red 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt walks with guards, as a narrator states, 'We walked into the gangs.' He gestures toward rows of heavily tattooed inmates in white uniforms seated on the floor, who respond '¡Buenas tardes!' to a call, surrounded by masked officers. The narrator explains, 'They locked them all up. Two gangs that had been terrorizing everyone. Now they're all in one place.' The scene cuts to an El Salvador cityscape with a prominent volcano, then to a news montage showing Nayib Bukele’s 2019 presidential victory, including his swearing-in ceremony and public speeches. The narrator highlights, 'This is what the President of El Salvador did. He changed the entire country... from being the world's crime capital for decades, to becoming one of the safest countries in the world today. All because of this president. This is Nayib Bukele.' The narrator further details Bukele's Arab and Palestinian descent. The video then shows Bukele addressing a crowd with soldiers in formation, followed by a nighttime prison scene with inmates being led by officers in riot gear. The segment ends with Bukele speaking in Spanish at a podium with El Salvador flags, stating 'These criminals of parliament...' with Arabic subtitles translating his remarks about parliamentary criminals and the need to guarantee public safety, as the narrator concludes, 'Since taking office in 2019, he has transformed the country.'

The segment opens with a news clip of Nayib Bukele, President of El Salvador, speaking at a podium with the Salvadoran flag, as a male voiceover translates his commitment to guaranteeing the safety of the people. The scene transitions to an aerial view of the 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' prison. A man in a red t-shirt, identified by his distinctive attire, stands outside the prison, gesturing expansively while stating, "This is Secot prison in El Salvador," and later adding, "It's one of the most secure prisons in the world, here in El Salvador," over soft female Spanish singing. The camera captures various angles of the prison exterior, including a guard and an arriving vehicle, before shifting to a first-person perspective from inside a van. The 'Man in red t-shirt' is visible in the back seat, engaging in conversation as the van drives along tree-lined roads, through rural landscapes, and into a cityscape with a prominent volcano, accompanied by car sounds and indistinct chatter. A different male voiceover mentions President Bukele's strong stance against gangs. The 'Man in red t-shirt' then explains, "Today we'll uncover the story of El Salvador and step inside Secot prison. We drove for an hour from the city to reach Secot," describing it as "one of the most heavily guarded prisons in the world." The segment concludes with a news montage showing armed soldiers during a state of emergency, with on-screen text "El Salvador records deadliest day in 30 years" and Arabic subtitles, as a male voiceover states, "In March 2022, El Salvador is under state of emergency," followed by another voiceover mentioning the declaration of a state of emergency. The final shot shows a high-security prison wall with a watchtower from a moving vehicle.

The video opens with an aerial view of the CECOT prison, a large, modern facility with high concrete walls, barbed wire, and a watchtower, set against green hills and farmland. A male narrator introduces it as a new prison, opened in 2023, built specifically for 64,000 gang members and known as the prison for gangsters and terrorists, all set to tense, orchestral background music. The camera moves to ground level, showing the entrance gate with the sign 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' and armed guards in camouflage uniforms. The narrator emphasizes the high level of security. A man in a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back exits a van, accompanied by car sounds, and walks toward the gate, gesturing. Inside the prison, he walks through a brightly lit corridor, passing by a sign for 'PERSONAL ADMINISTRATIVO' and later 'CONTROL DE ACCESO'. He is heard speaking in a foreign language, translated by Arabic subtitles as "There's no signal around. All the area." and later "Hello, how are you?". He enters a security checkpoint area with metal detectors and X-ray machines, monitored by masked prison guards in blue uniforms. The narrator explains that media outlets were invited to broadcast the scene as a deterrent. A map of El Salvador briefly highlights the CECOT location before returning to the man inside the prison, where he is greeted by a guard who states, "Welcome. I'm the director of the Center of Confinement of Terrorism." The narrator concludes that the security measures are so tight it feels like going through an airport, with strict screening before entry, as the man places his belongings on a table for inspection, filmed by a camera operator.

The minute begins with a low humming sound as a Man in a red t-shirt approaches a security checkpoint inside the CECOT prison, featuring a large 'Intercept' metal detector that glows red as he passes through. A narrator comments on the "advanced equipment," stating this prison is "not about rehabilitation" but "strictly for gang members." A Masked prison guard in a light blue uniform greets the man, gesturing him forward. The man hands over his phone and ID at a metal table for scanning. The narrator notes, "There are two more gates here," and identifies this as "the second checkpoint" and a "special inspection room." As the man proceeds through a sterile, brightly lit corridor, the narrator explains, "They check clothing and even socks; everything must be lightweight." Another Masked prison guard then conducts a thorough physical pat-down, checking his arms, back, and legs, and inspecting his white Nike sneakers, while a voice, possibly a guard, says "Comenzamos" (Let's begin) and "Vamos" (Let's go). The man remains calm and cooperative throughout. He continues walking, passing signs for 'CONTROL DE ACCESO 1' and 'SANITARIO', then exits into a covered outdoor area. Accompanied by a guard, he walks towards a door marked 'PRIVADOS DE LIBERTAD', re-entering the facility past a sign reading 'OBLIGATORIO USO DE ALCOHOL GEL'. The narrator observes, "We are entering another room now," and questions, "How many rooms do we have to go through before reaching the prisoners?" The entire process is filmed by a visible camera operator with a stabilizer, within the clean, institutional environment characterized by fluorescent lighting and minimal decoration.

The video opens with an aerial view of the vast, modern CECOT prison, a facility with high concrete walls and multiple buildings, surrounded by greenery and water, emphasizing its isolated and secure location. A male narrator with a clear, authoritative voice explains that CECOT consists of 'eight massive cell blocks,' each capable of holding 'thousands of inmates,' calling it a 'security fortress.' Inside, the Man in red t-shirt, wearing a 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt, undergoes a thorough security screening. He passes through a 'Tek84 Intercept' metal detector, which the narrator identifies as a 'high-tech device' used to verify both prisoners and staff. He then hands over his phone and ID at a security table before Masked prison guards, dressed in black uniforms with balaclavas, perform a physical pat-down. The camera operator films from behind as the man proceeds through a brightly lit corridor lined with cells, where Tattooed inmates in white uniforms are seated on the floor under guard. Spanish signs like 'CONTROL DE ACCESO 1' and 'OBLIGATORIO USO DE ALCOHOL GEL' reinforce the strict environment. The narrator continues, detailing advanced cameras with facial recognition and motion sensors used for verification, especially for criminals arriving without identification. The man enters a large room labeled 'AFILIACIÓN' and gestures, then walks toward a covered outdoor area. Here, a Guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve is seen speaking to him, as the narrator explains that CECOT was built in a record 'seven months' to control dangerous criminals and meet all requirements for managing such subjects, with the CECOT logo visible on the gate. Throughout, a low, suspenseful background music underscores the serious atmosphere.

The segment opens with a wide shot of the CECOT prison's single entrance, a long, covered driveway flanked by high walls and a metal roof, with signage for 'CECOT - Centro de Confinamiento del Terrorismo' and 'CONTROL DE ACCESO 1'. An informative male narrator states that "many years caused mourning and pain to our society," and describes the facility's "single entrance, entry and exit." The scene transitions to a conversation where the Man in red t-shirt, with a slightly accented voice, asks a Guard in black uniform, "why was C-cot born?" The narrator explains that "Cecot was built following the declaration of a state of emergency, with mass arrests taking place," necessitating "a vast facility... to house the criminals." As an aerial night view of the massive, blue-lit, grid-like CECOT complex is shown, the narrator emphasizes it is "the largest prison in Latin America, spanning the size of eight football fields with a capacity of 40,000 inmates." A map of El Salvador then highlights the country's location, with animated markers for "Barrio 18 and MS-13," which the narrator identifies as "two of the largest gangs in Latin America" now "behind bars." Chaotic scenes of Tattooed inmates being subdued by Masked prison guards in a corridor, with one inmate running bare-chested, visually reinforce the prison's purpose. The Man in red t-shirt is later seen walking through the prison grounds, speaking to the camera, while the Guard walks ahead, with the Man noting, "Very few people are granted permission. I might be the first Arab to receive permission to film inside." He then asks, "How difficult is to escape this prison?" The narrator responds, "Impossible," as the segment ends with a close-up of a watchtower and barbed wire fencing, detailing the prison is "surrounded by massive, 15-meter high concrete walls, reinforced with barbed wire and 19 watchtowers." Arabic and English subtitles provide additional context throughout.

The segment opens with a narrator stating that escaping from the CECOT prison is impossible due to its extreme security, guarded by 600 soldiers and 250 police officers, as the Man in red t-shirt is escorted by a Masked prison guard along a paved outdoor path under a clear blue sky. They pass a high-security building with a watchtower and barbed wire, then enter a covered walkway, proceeding down a brightly lit corridor with blue-painted lower walls, passing other guards. The narrator announces, 'We're going now to the armory,' echoing on-screen text in Arabic and English. The guard leads the man to a room marked 'ARMERÍA' (Armory) under the CECOT logo. Inside the sterile, well-lit armory, rows of black rifles are displayed on metal racks. The narrator continues, 'This is an armory here,' warning that anyone thinking of escaping should 'just look at what awaits them,' and mentions El Salvador declared war on gangs. As the guard demonstrates a firearm to the man, who gestures and asks questions, the narrator reiterates, 'Here we are in the weapons warehouse.' Background music intensifies, and a distinct voice (possibly a guard or another narrator) states, 'So you are announce a war against the gang,' followed by, 'Yes, we have this other type of weaponry,' as close-ups show the rifles and a table with handcuffs and chains, emphasizing the armory's function and the prison's strict security protocols.

event_blocks.summary: Inside the CECOT prison's weapons warehouse, a masked prison guard in a black uniform displayed various armaments to a Man in a red t-shirt, explaining that these 'weapons' are part of a 'war on terrorism' to enhance national safety and emphasizing the necessity of 'having them under control.' The guard demonstrated restraints, including 'cuffs' for 'hands' and 'leg cuffs' for 'feet,' illustrating how they 'bind both hands and feet together,' impeding movement and causing individuals to 'walk uncomfortably.' The Man in red t-shirt observed intently, examining handcuffs and chains, and reacted with surprise when the guard presented a large metal chain, followed by the guard holding a baton identified as 'The Tonfa.' Atmospheric music began as the scene shifted to the man positioned between two heavily armored officers in riot gear, whose 'equipment' was described as 'designed for emergencies' and a 'result of the state of emergency,' intended to protect citizens with 'public safety is the priority.' Exiting the armory, the group proceeded through a corridor and into the prison yard, then to a vehicle. The perspective changed to a first-person view from inside a car driving towards the high-security prison complex, passing a watchtower and barbed wire fencing, accompanied by engine and road noise. The journey continued past the prison’s white and blue exterior under a bright, sunlit sky with distant mountains, culminating in arrival at a covered area where the man was greeted by another guard amidst a ringing phone and fading music. Subsequently, the Man in red t-shirt, wearing a 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt, ascended concrete stairs outside a building marked 'COMEDOR AGENTES,' escorted by a masked prison guard in a black uniform with 'JUSTICIA' and 'CECOT'/'EL SALVADOR' insignia. Entering a large, empty dining hall, the guard clarified the facility was 'for the guards, not the inmates,' stating 'the prisoners cannot leave their cells.' The guard inquired if the man felt 'responsible of the safety of the country,' to which the man responded affirmatively, 'Yes, this is what makes a country safe,' and thanked the guard. Moving through a covered outdoor corridor with a mirror and a wall-mounted fan, the man briefly checked his reflection as the guard explained that even shaving facilities were 'for the guards' who 'must be well taken care of since they're surrounded by some of the most dangerous criminals in the world.' They entered a dormitory furnished with metal bunk beds, identified by the guard as 'the guards' dormitories,' detailing their 'five days on, five days off' schedule designed 'to minimize the impact of stress.' The man engaged in a light workout with a dumbbell while the guard described the CECOT prison as consistently bright, institutional, clean, organized, and secure.

Weapons warehouse.description: A secure, well-lit room within the CECOT prison containing organized racks of rifles and other restraints, marked by signage as 'ARMERÍA', serving as the storage and distribution point for prison armaments, where the guard's voice explains the purpose and function of the various security tools.

Masked prison guard.description: A uniformed officer in black attire, wearing a cap with 'JUSTICIA' and 'CECOT'/'EL SALVADOR' insignia, and sunglasses, escorting the man through the prison. He speaks in English, explaining the purpose of various facilities (dining hall, shaving area, dormitories) as being exclusively for guards, not inmates, and detailing the guards' work schedule and the psychological impact of their duties.

Watchtower and barbed wire.description: A concrete watchtower with a guard on the balcony, surrounded by multiple layers of barbed wire fencing, emphasizing the prison's security measures, which are seen from a car driving past with accompanying engine and road noise.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, being escorted through the CECOT prison, observing facilities, gesturing, smiling, and engaging in conversation with the guard, expressing gratitude for their service and acknowledging their role in national safety.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in black uniforms, some wearing balaclavas and riot gear with 'DGCP' shields, conducting security checks and escorting the man. Their 'equipment' is described by the guard's voice as 'designed for emergencies' to 'protecting citizens.'

Rifles.description: Multiple black rifles displayed on metal racks in the armory, with close-ups showing their design and handling by the guard, which are contextually described by the guard's voice as part of the 'weapons' used in the 'war on terrorism.'

CECOT prison.description: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, featuring well-lit corridors, a dining hall, and dormitories. The guard's voice emphasizes its secure nature and explains that its facilities are specifically designed for the well-being and operational needs of the guards, given their dangerous environment.

Handcuffs and chains.description: Metal handcuffs and chains laid out on a black table in the armory, examined by the man, and described by the guard as 'cuffs' for 'hands' and 'leg cuffs' for 'feet' that 'bind both hands and feet together,' causing individuals to 'walk uncomfortably.'

Dormitory.description: A spacious room with multiple metal bunk beds, white mattresses, and a weight rack with dumbbells. The Masked prison guard's voice identifies these as 'the guards' dormitories,' explaining their 'five days on, five days off' schedule designed 'to minimize the impact of stress' from their duties.

Wall-mounted fan.description: A black oscillating fan mounted on a wooden bracket on a white wall in a covered corridor, providing ventilation in the facility.

Dining hall.description: A large, empty room with rows of black tables and chairs, white walls, ceiling fans, and bright fluorescent lighting. The Masked prison guard's voice explicitly states this area is 'for the guards, not the inmates'.

Signage.description: Spanish signs including 'COMEDOR AGENTES' above the dining hall entrance and 'COCINA' on a door in the hallway, visually indicating facility functions.

raw_segments: The video opens inside the CECOT prison's weapons warehouse, where a Masked prison guard, in a black uniform, shows a Man in red t-shirt various armaments. A male voice, likely the guard, explains that these 'weapons' are part of a 'war on terrorism' to make the country safer, emphasizing the need to 'have them under control.' The guard points out different restraints, including 'cuffs' for 'hands' and 'leg cuffs' for 'feet,' demonstrating how 'these bind both hands and feet together,' causing individuals to 'walk uncomfortably.' The Man in red t-shirt listens intently, examining handcuffs and chains, reacting with surprise as the guard demonstrates a large metal chain. The guard then holds a baton, identified by the voice as 'The Tonfa.' Tense, atmospheric music begins as the scene transitions to the man standing between two heavily armored officers in riot gear, whose 'equipment' is explained as 'designed for emergencies' and a 'result of the state of emergency,' serving to protect citizens as 'public safety is the priority.' The group exits the armory, walking through a corridor and into the prison yard. The camera follows them to a vehicle, and the perspective shifts to a first-person view from inside a car. The vehicle drives along a road towards the modern, high-security prison complex, passing a watchtower and barbed wire fencing, with the distinct sound of the engine and road noise accompanying the journey. The drive continues past the prison’s white and blue exterior, under bright, sunlit skies with distant mountains, before arriving at a covered area where the man is greeted by another guard, as a phone rings in the background and the music fades.

The video opens with the Man in red t-shirt, wearing a 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' t-shirt, ascending concrete stairs outside a modern, industrial-style building marked 'COMEDOR AGENTES'. He is escorted by a Masked prison guard in a black uniform, cap with 'JUSTICIA', and 'CECOT'/'EL SALVADOR' insignia. The guard's voice explains that the facility, including the large, empty dining hall they enter, is "for the guards, not the inmates," adding that "the prisoners cannot leave their cells." As they converse, with Arabic subtitles translating dialogue, the guard asks the man if he feels "responsible of the safety of the country," to which the man replies, "Yes, this is what makes a country safe," before thanking the guard for his service. The man then walks through a covered outdoor corridor with a mirror and a wall-mounted fan, briefly checking his reflection, as the guard's voice clarifies that even the shaving facilities are "for the guards" who "must be well taken care of since they're surrounded by some of the most dangerous criminals in the world." They proceed down a long, sterile hallway past a 'COCINA' door and enter a dormitory filled with metal bunk beds. The guard's voice identifies these as "the guards' dormitories," explaining their "five days on, five days off" schedule designed "to minimize the impact of stress." The man picks up a dumbbell and engages in a light workout, while the guard continues to detail the environment, highlighting the CECOT prison's consistently bright, institutional, clean, organized, and secure nature.

event_blocks.summary: Inside the CECOT prison, a Man in a red t-shirt tours the facility, initially observing a gym for guards before proceeding into the high-security prisoner area. He passes a chain-link gate labeled 'ZONA 2 MÓDULOS 3-4 CECOT' and is informed by a Masked prison guard that "The world's most dangerous gang leaders are just a few steps away" and "Once they pass through, through this gate, they never come out." The tour continues through a covered corridor marked 'MÓDULO 4 CECOT' and into a long, brightly lit prison hallway lined with barred cells, accompanied by the sound of a gate. The man walks down the corridor, greeting guards with "Good evening" as he passes other guards and Tattooed inmates in white uniforms, some with visible tattoos on their heads and arms, observing intensely. The narrator explains that inmates are from different gangs, imprisoned together despite their affiliations, and have committed numerous violent crimes, with no family visits allowed. The strict regimen includes lights on "24/7" for constant monitoring, and inmates sleep on metal surfaces without mattresses or blankets, denied outdoor exercise beyond 30 minutes daily. Masked prison guards in gray uniforms and balaclavas conduct security checks, using handcuffs and performing physical checks. Inmates are sometimes directed to sit on the floor in a semi-circle or perform synchronized movements, chanting or singing Spanish hymns like "Oh lamento y clamor, abre los cielos sobre nosotros, abre los cielos, Señor, a llover" and "Somos tu pueblo, somos tu vida, abre los cielos, Señor," guided by an instructor counting aloud in Spanish. Close-ups reveal intricate tattoos, including 'MS-13' on one inmate's forehead, with the narrator noting that tattoos reveal gang affiliation, which is cataloged by police. The constant low hum of the facility, shuffling sounds, metallic clanks, and tense background music underscore the controlled, high-security environment.

Masked prison guard.description: A uniformed officer in black attire, wearing a cap and sunglasses, who serves as the primary speaker and guide, explaining the purpose of the gym ('This gym is for the guards. It's all for the guards' well-being,' and coping 'from being there in front of those criminals all day'), guiding the man ('Come with me. Now we're about to enter the prisoners' area. This is the most important part of the video.'), emphasizing the high-security nature of the CECOT prison ('The world's most dangerous gang leaders are just a few steps away. Once they enter the Center of Confinement of Terrorism, they will never leave again. Once they pass through, through this gate, they never come out.'), and directing the man further into the facility ('We're now going to the cells. Come inside.'). He escorts the man through the CECOT prison.

Prison corridor.description: A long, brightly lit hallway with a curved ceiling, lined with barred cells on both sides, marked by yellow floor lines and cell numbers (e.g., 15, 16), serving as the primary setting for the inmates’ ritualistic gathering and performance.

Guard in black uniform.description: A prison guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve, speaking to the man in the red t-shirt in a covered outdoor area, explaining the construction and purpose of the CECOT prison.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, being escorted through the CECOT prison corridor, gesturing and engaging in conversation with a guard who explains the facility's 24/7 monitoring and inmate conditions.

CECOT prison.description: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, featuring well-lit corridors, specific signage like 'ZONA 2 MÓDULOS 3-4 CECOT' and 'MÓDULO 4 CECOT', and a hallway marked 'CELDAS'. The speaker emphasizes its purpose as the Center of Confinement of Terrorism, where 'the world's most dangerous gang leaders' are held and from which 'they will never leave again'.

Signage.description: Spanish signs including 'COMEDOR AGENTES' above the dining hall entrance and 'COCINA' on a door in the hallway, indicating facility functions and restricted zones.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in gray shirts and black balaclavas, standing along the corridor with batons, observing the inmates’ movements, maintaining a vigilant and controlled presence throughout the sequence.

Tattooed inmates.description: Inmates in white uniforms and blue surgical masks, many with extensive tattoos on arms and heads, initially seated in a semi-circle, then standing in formation to perform synchronized gestures, including raising arms and pointing forward, under the leadership of a central figure. They are heard chanting in Spanish, 'Somos tu pueblo, somos tu vida, abre los cielos, Señor' (We are your people, we are your life, open the heavens, Lord), and performing movements to spoken commands and counts from an instructor. Their visible tattoos are noted by a narrator as indicators of gang affiliation, which is cataloged by police and can lead to immediate arrest, with some known to attempt altering them.

raw_segments: The video opens inside a well-lit gym with various exercise equipment, where a Man in red t-shirt is seen lifting dumbbells. A Masked prison guard in black uniform, who serves as the speaker, explains that "This gym is for the guards. It's all for the guards' well-being," implying it helps them cope "from being there in front of those criminals all day." As background music begins, the man and the guard exit the building, walking along a sunlit outdoor path toward a high-security facility. The speaker invites, "Come with me. Now we're about to enter the prisoners' area. This is the most important part of the video." They pass a chain-link gate topped with razor wire, labeled 'ZONA 2 MÓDULOS 3-4 CECOT', indicating the Center of Confinement of Terrorism. The guard leads the man through the gate, with the speaker emphasizing that "The world's most dangerous gang leaders are just a few steps away," and that "Once they enter the Center of Confinement of Terrorism, they will never leave again. Once they pass through, through this gate, they never come out." The man points toward a large white building with a curved roof, and the camera pans to show guards on an upper balcony. The scene transitions to the man being escorted into a covered corridor with a sign reading 'MÓDULO 4 CECOT', then into a long, brightly lit prison hallway lined with barred cells, accompanied by the distinct sound of a gate. The speaker states, "We're now going to the cells," and then, "Come inside. We walked into the gangs..." The hallway is marked with a sign 'CELDAS' above the entrance, and the man walks down the corridor, greeting guards with 'Good evening' as he passes. Other guards and inmates are visible in the background, some standing near cell doors, while the man continues walking, observing the structured and controlled, high-security environment of the CECOT prison.

The video opens with a wide shot of a long, brightly lit prison corridor, featuring a curved ceiling with linear lighting, yellow floor markings, and barred cells on both sides, some marked with numbers like '7' and '31'. A man in a red t-shirt, with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on his back, walks down the hallway, flanked by numerous masked prison guards in black uniforms. As he proceeds, the man greets the surroundings with a clear "Buenas tardes!", which is met by a collective "Buenas tardes!" response, likely from the guards and inmates. Inside the cells, numerous tattooed inmates, wearing white uniforms, T-shirts, white shorts, and white slippers—described by the narrator as their only possessions—are seen standing or sitting, observing with intense gazes. The narrator explains that none of their family members are allowed to visit, and that these inmates, from different gangs, are imprisoned together, having participated in the murder of over 200 people, rape, and many other crimes. Close-ups reveal their detailed tattoos on heads and arms, some inmates resting on metal surfaces, as the narrator notes they sleep on these iron surfaces due to a lack of mattresses. The narrator further describes that if one looks at them, they will give a "terrifying glare," and emphasizes the "huge number of guards" present, making it feel "like the country is at war." The man in red continues walking, occasionally turning to look at the inmates, while the guards maintain a vigilant presence in this modern, high-security environment, all prisoners uniformly dressed in white. Throughout, a low ambient hum and subtle, tense background music underscore the controlled and high-stakes atmosphere.

The segment opens with a wide shot of the brightly lit, high-security CECOT prison corridor, featuring a curved ceiling, linear lighting, and barred cells. A Man in a red t-shirt is escorted through this sterile environment by a Masked prison guard in a black uniform, who gestures and explains the facility's operations. The guard, speaking with an accent, emphasizes that "Control and monitoring is 24/7" and that a camera system allows real-time monitoring of all activities, a statement reinforced by Arabic and English subtitles. A narrator's voice further details the strict regimen, explaining that the lights are never turned off, staying on "24/7" so "prisoners never know the time and the guards can monitor them," preventing misbehavior. Close-ups reveal Tattooed inmates inside the cells, many wearing blue surgical masks and displaying intricate head tattoos, some looking intensely at the camera. The narrator adds that each cell, despite a capacity of 100, only has 80 beds, and inmates are denied blankets. The scene shifts back to the corridor as the Man in red t-shirt continues his tour, listening intently. The camera then focuses on a group of Masked prison guards in gray uniforms and balaclavas conducting a security check on inmates inside a cell, using handcuffs and performing physical checks amidst faint clanking sounds. The inmates, in white uniforms and masks, sit or stand behind bars, some with visible arm and leg tattoos, as guards methodically secure them. The narrator clarifies that "prisoners are only allowed out of their cells for 30 minutes a day to exercise, using only their body weight." The constant low hum of the facility underscores the tense, highly structured atmosphere, with yellow floor lines marking pathways and guards stationed at intervals, emphasizing the prison's strict control.

The video opens in a brightly lit, modern prison corridor, featuring a curved ceiling and rows of barred cells marked by a 'CELDAS' sign. The constant shuffling and movement sounds underscore the activity as a male narrative voice states, "The cells are at full capacity." Masked prison guards in gray uniforms and black balaclavas are conducting security checks. One guard, with visible arm tattoos, is actively handcuffing an inmate in a white uniform and blue surgical mask, who raises his hands as he is led out of a cell. Other inmates, similarly dressed and masked, watch from behind the bars. The narrative voice comments, "It feels like a scene from a movie," followed by the word "Secot." Guards maintain a vigilant presence, one holding a baton, as the handcuffed inmate is escorted down the corridor. More guards and inmates are visible in the background, some standing in line or observing, contributing to the tense and controlled atmosphere. The corridor features yellow floor markings and a clean, industrial aesthetic, illuminated by bright overhead lighting. Throughout the segment, the deliberate and coordinated movements of the guards ensure order. Towards the end, muffled, indistinct speech is heard, accompanied by a distinct metallic clank or rattle, possibly from a gate or cell bars, further emphasizing the strict security protocols.

The video opens with a dynamic, handheld tracking shot moving through a long, brightly lit, modern CECOT prison corridor, characterized by its curved ceiling and rows of barred cells. The distinct sounds of footsteps and indistinct Spanish commands like "Vamos" accompany the movement as masked prison guards in gray uniforms and black balaclavas escort a line of inmates in white uniforms and blue surgical masks, some with visible tattoos on their arms and heads. A loud metallic clang and a sustained, low hum resonate through the corridor, emphasizing the facility's security as the procession continues. The guards maintain tight control, some holding batons and others guiding inmates by the arms, as the inmates are led in a single file along a yellow line on the floor. Other inmates observe silently from behind the bars of the cells. The camera follows closely, capturing the tense atmosphere and disciplined movement. As the hum fades, the inmates are directed to sit on the floor in a semi-circle, their hands either on their knees or restrained, accompanied by shuffling sounds. The camera zooms in on several tattooed inmates, highlighting intricate designs on their heads and necks, including one with 'MS-13' tattooed on his forehead. As they sit, a group of male voices, presumably the inmates, begins to sing a Spanish hymn in unison, "Oh lamento y clamor, abre los cielos sobre nosotros, abre los cielos, Señor, a llover," filling the sterile, controlled environment. Guards stand vigilantly around the seated group under the cool, clinical lighting. The sequence ends with a wide shot showing the full group of seated inmates, surrounded by guards, with the corridor stretching into the distance, reinforcing the scale and security of the facility, as a narrator states, "This is the only activity they're allowed."

The minute opens in a long, brightly lit corridor of the CECOT prison, featuring barred cells, a curved ceiling with linear lighting, and a yellow floor line with visible cell numbers. Somber instrumental music plays as a narrator states, "They're not allowed to do anything else." A group of tattooed inmates in white uniforms and blue surgical masks are initially seated in a semi-circle, facing a central leader, also tattooed and in white. They begin to chant in Spanish, "Somos tu pueblo, somos tu vida, abre los cielos, Señor" (We are your people, we are your life, open the heavens, Lord), a phrase also shown in Arabic subtitles. Following a "Gloria a Dios" and "Diga a Dios" from an instructor, the inmates transition to standing in precise formation, performing synchronized movements like raising arms and pointing forward, guided by an instructor counting aloud in Spanish ("Uno, dos, tres... diez") and giving commands like "Mano izquierda" (Left hand). Masked prison guards in gray uniforms and black balaclavas stand vigilantly along the corridor, some holding batons, observing the disciplined yet tense scene. Close-ups highlight the inmates’ tattooed limbs and white Crocs. A narrator explains that the inmates' tattoos reveal their gang affiliation, which is cataloged by police and leads to immediate arrest, noting that some try to alter these markings. The inmates continue their coordinated movements, with further counting heard from the instructor.

event_blocks.summary: In a brightly lit CECOT prison corridor, tattooed inmates in white uniforms and blue masks execute synchronized exercises, their grunts and chants punctuated by the words "Sí" and "Suave," while masked prison guards in gray shirts and black balaclavas observe. A medical staff member in blue scrubs stands near a cell as a Guard in black uniform informs a Man in red t-shirt that nurses and a doctor are present daily, emphasizing that medical staff must remain due to inmates' inability to leave the area. A metallic clanging sound accompanies the Man in red t-shirt's question, "From here?" as they pass the 'ÁREA MÉDICA' sign and enter a narrow corridor leading to a room featuring a large screen with a court interface. The Guard explains that inmates communicate with lawyers and attend trials via video calls in this facility, as they are not permitted to leave, and that they can "visualize the court and the other prisoners that are linked to the same hearing" through the screen. While walking back past the clinic, the Guard reiterates its presence and concludes by remarking on the numerous rooms within the prison, all underscored by low, ominous background music.

Prison corridor.description: A long, brightly lit hallway with a curved ceiling, lined with barred cells on both sides, marked by yellow floor lines, serving as the primary setting for the inmates’ ritualistic gathering and performance, and the escort route, where a metallic clanging sound is heard near the medical area.

Screen interface.description: A large wall-mounted screen displaying a digital court interface with options like 'Welcome', 'Start new hearing', and 'Select your hearing', which the guard explains allows inmates to visualize the court and other linked prisoners for hearings.

Guard in black uniform.description: A prison guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve, speaking in a clear male voice, explaining the presence of nurses and daily doctor visits, the restriction on inmates leaving, the function of the video call room for lawyers and trials, the court visualization screen, and later pointing out the clinic and other rooms within the 'incredible prison'.

Tattooed inmates.description: Inmates in white uniforms and blue surgical masks, many with extensive tattoos on arms and heads, performing synchronized gestures in a brightly lit corridor, accompanied by rhythmic grunting and chanting sounds, with a distinct 'Sí' and 'Suave' heard.

Court visualization room.description: A small, plain-walled room with a large screen displaying a court interface, where the guard explains inmates can communicate with lawyers via video call, conduct trials, and visualize the court and other linked prisoners.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, being escorted through the CECOT prison corridor, asking 'From here?' when passing the medical area, and later gesturing towards a screen while listening to the guard's explanations about the court visualization room.

Medical staff member.description: A person in blue scrubs and a hairnet, standing near a cell, interacting with inmates and overseeing medical procedures, while the guard explains the constant presence of medical staff.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in gray shirts and black balaclavas, standing along the corridor with batons, observing the inmates’ movements, maintaining a vigilant and controlled presence.

Signage.description: Spanish signs including 'ÁREA MÉDICA' above the medical area entrance and 'COCINA' on a door in the hallway, indicating facility functions and restricted zones, with the 'ÁREA MÉDICA' sign being passed as the guard explains medical services.

raw_segments: The video opens in a brightly lit CECOT prison corridor, where tattooed inmates in white uniforms and blue masks perform synchronized physical exercises, accompanied by rhythmic grunting and chanting, with a distinct "Sí" and "Suave" heard. Masked prison guards in gray shirts and black balaclavas vigilantly observe. The scene transitions to a medical staff member in blue scrubs near a cell, as a Guard in black uniform, speaking in a clear male voice, explains to the Man in red t-shirt that "There are nurses here" and "A doctor visits daily to check on them," adding that "Medical staff must always be present because inmates are not allowed to leave from here." A metallic clanging sound is heard as the Man in red t-shirt asks, "From here?" while passing the 'ÁREA MÉDICA' sign. They then enter a narrow corridor leading to a small room with a large screen displaying a court interface. The Guard continues, explaining, "There's a room where they can communicate with their lawyers via video call," and "Trials are conducted here because they're not allowed to go out. Here they speak to their lawyers via video." He further elaborates, "Here we seat the inmates through the screen, we can visualize the court and the other prisoners that are linked to the same hearing," as the Man in red t-shirt gestures towards the screen, with Arabic subtitles translating the explanation. As they walk back, passing the clinic, the Guard points out, "Here we have the clinic," and concludes, "Look at all these rooms inside this incredible prison," all set against a backdrop of low, slightly ominous background music.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative unfolds within the stark, brightly lit corridors and cells of the CECOT prison, where tattooed inmates, members of dangerous gangs like Barrio 18 and MS-13, are confined. A Man in a red t-shirt, identified by 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' on his back, acts as a guide and narrator, accompanied by a camera operator and a woman with a microphone, while masked prison guards in gray uniforms and black balaclavas maintain a vigilant presence. The narrator details the inmates' crimes, including aggravated homicide, kidnapping, and murder, often highlighting their extensive tattoos which serve as identifiers and symbolic representations of their violent past, including names, dates, skulls, tombstones, and images of those they killed or lost. Specific inmates are profiled, such as Elias El Seco, charged with aggravated homicide and facing over 100 years, and an MS-13 member who confessed to killing 30 people. The environment is characterized by the clanking of chains, the starkness of cell interiors with only cement slabs, and the institutional routine of food distribution, where meals of beans and rice are delivered directly to cells by a worker in a hairnet pushing a food cart. The Man in red t-shirt explains that inmates are confined to their cells, which also serve as their toilets, and that solitary confinement cells are used for punishment. Arabic subtitles appear throughout, translating key phrases and inmate confessions, underscoring the gravity of their crimes and their remorse, with one inmate stating he cries every day but it's too late. The overall atmosphere is tense, controlled, and somber, emphasizing the high-security nature of the facility and the grim reality of the inmates' lives.

Prison corridor.description: A long, brightly lit hallway with pale green walls, marked by a sign reading 'CELDA DE AISLAMIENTO' and 'CECOT', lined with barred cells and featuring a metal door, serving as the primary setting for the inmates’ inspection and processing.

Camera operator.description: A person holding a professional camera with a stabilizer, filming the man in the red t-shirt as he goes through the security checkpoint inside the CECOT prison, visible in the background during the process.

Guard in black uniform.description: A prison guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve, holding a clipboard and interacting with inmates, inspecting their tattoos and overseeing the processing procedure, speaking to the man in the red t-shirt.

Tattooed inmates.description: Inmates in white uniforms, many with extensive tattoos on their chests, arms, and heads, including names like 'Santana Guzmán', dates, and symbolic imagery like skulls and women’s faces. They are seen sitting on the floor in a line, some lifting their shirts to reveal tattoos, with handcuffs and chains visible on their wrists and ankles. One specific inmate is identified by a voiceover as an 'MS-13 member' and is heard responding in Spanish to questions about his past actions and regrets, with an English voiceover summarizing his remorse.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, seen being escorted through the CECOT prison corridor, gesturing and engaging in conversation with the guard, pointing at tattoos on inmates. He is heard asking the inmate about the number of people he killed and if he regrets his past actions.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in gray shirts and black balaclavas, standing along the corridor with batons, observing the inmates’ movements, maintaining a vigilant and controlled presence throughout the sequence.

Handcuffs and chains.description: Metal handcuffs and chains securing the wrists and ankles of inmates, visible during close-ups as they lift their shirts to reveal tattoos, and audibly clanking, especially towards the end of the segment, emphasizing the physical restraints used in the prison.

Arabic subtitles.description: On-screen text in Arabic, appearing during the close-up of a somber inmate, translating to 'Do you regret what you did?' and 'How do you feel about your punishment?', adding a narrative layer to the visual content and aligning with the interviewer's spoken questions.

Cell interior.description: A dark, bare cell with concrete walls and floor, containing only a cement slab, a sink, and a yellow bucket, illuminated by a flashlight held by the Man in red t-shirt.

Worker in hairnet.description: A person wearing a white hairnet, gloves, and a white shirt, pushing the food cart and handing out meals to inmates through the bars.

Food distribution cart.description: A metal cart stacked with clear plastic containers filled with rice, beans, and bread, being pushed by a worker in a hairnet and gloves, used to distribute meals to inmates through the bars.

Food container.description: Clear plastic containers with lids, stacked on a cart, filled with layers of rice, beans, and bread, used for meal distribution to inmates. The narrator explicitly states the contents are 'beans and rice, no chicken, no meat'.

CECOT prison.description: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, signage reading 'ARMERÍA' and 'CECOT', featuring well-lit corridors and a weapons warehouse with organized racks of rifles.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a wide shot of a stark prison corridor, where a group of tattooed inmates in white uniforms are seated in a semi-circle on the floor. A narrator's voice immediately sets the scene, stating, "We are now walking among members of the two most dangerous gangs in the world." The Man in red t-shirt, wearing a distinctive maroon shirt, walks through the corridor, observing the inmates, accompanied by a camera operator and a woman with a microphone. Masked prison guards stand vigilantly along the walls. The narrator continues, "I'm going to present to you three profiles of the type of criminals we have here," describing them as "subjects who... played at being gods. They decided who lived or who died." Tense, dramatic background music begins as the Man in red t-shirt engages in conversation with a Guard in black uniform. The camera then focuses on a specific inmate, handcuffed and chained, with visible tattoos. The narrator instructs, "Take off your mask and tell us about their crimes. They were such a danger to society." The narrator identifies this inmate as "Elias El Seco, active member of the gang Barrio 18," adding that he is "charged with aggravated homicide" and "has a sentence of more than 100 years in prison." The narrator then commands, "Lift up your shirt," and the inmate complies, revealing more extensive tattoos, including a large design on his side, as the narrator concludes, "Their tattoos will tell us..."

The segment opens in a stark, brightly lit CECOT prison corridor with pale green walls, where inmates in white uniforms are processed by masked prison guards in gray uniforms and black balaclavas, who stand vigilantly with batons. A narrator immediately begins detailing the inmates' crimes, mentioning the "gang they belong to." The focus shifts to a heavily tattooed inmate, lifting his shirt to reveal intricate designs, as the narrator states, "This one killed five people... just because they crossed into his territory." A command to "Turn around" is heard, likely from a guard. The narrator then generalizes that "They all have tattoos" before highlighting another heavily tattooed inmate with a goatee, identifying him as "an MS-13 member" who "kidnapped four people." The narrator continues, describing "The one beside him" who "murdered five," emphasizing that "This is just a small sample of the criminals imprisoned here." A man in a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back is seen gesturing to a guard in a black uniform holding a clipboard. The narrator then introduces "The third inmate," also a gang member, who "killed a young girl who had a promising future as an athlete." As a somber inmate with a shaved head and arm tattoo is shown in close-up, Arabic subtitles appear stating 'and later murdered her,' a detail immediately echoed by the narrator who adds, "He later murdered her," and "He stole her future." Another command, "Lift up your shirt," is heard. The scene transitions to a wider shot of inmates kneeling in a line in a large, open hall with barred windows, under the watchful eyes of guards, with the distinct, heavy clanking of chains audible, underscoring the tense, controlled, and high-security environment.

The segment opens with a stark close-up of heavily tattooed inmates, their bodies intricately adorned with names, dates, and symbolic imagery like skulls and women’s faces, seated on the floor of a brightly lit prison corridor. A voiceover immediately explains that "their tattoos made it easier to identify and capture them," noting that an "18 tattoo" signifies a "Barrio 18 member." The camera pans across the group, revealing their shaved heads and white prison uniforms, while masked prison guards in gray shirts and black balaclavas stand vigilantly. A man in a red t-shirt, bearing 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' on his back, walks through the corridor, gesturing and conversing with a guard in a black uniform. The voiceover continues, detailing that "most of the tattoos... have some tombstones," worn "in memory of other gang members, members of their clique who were eliminated," and also include "tattoos of graves and skulls of people they've killed." As the man in red points to specific tattoos, a close-up reveals detailed artwork, including a tombstone with 'Santana Guzmán' and 'R.I.P.', which the voiceover identifies as "his grandmother." Another tattoo is identified as "his son's face." The guard in black, holding a clipboard, inspects the tattoos. The voiceover further clarifies that these tattoos are "in memory of fallen gang members," often depicted as skulls, and "every tattoo has a meaning." The modern, high-security facility, with barred cells marked '19' and '13' and a yellow demarcation line, maintains a somber and controlled atmosphere. The segment concludes with the man in red continuing his conversation, the inmates remaining serious and reflective, as a female vocal track begins to play, and the voiceover abruptly states, "They arrested them all and locked..."

The video opens in a brightly lit, high-security CECOT prison corridor, where a line of tattooed inmates in white uniforms are seated on the floor. A Man in a red t-shirt, identified by 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' on his back, walks through, accompanied by a Guard in a black uniform. The Man in red t-shirt provides continuous commentary, explaining that these inmates belong to two gangs that were once at war but are now imprisoned together. As the Guard points out specific tattoos, the Man in red t-shirt describes common designs like women's faces on their legs, skulls, and marijuana imagery, which are also highlighted by Arabic subtitles. The camera pans across the inmates, revealing extensive tattoos including names like 'Santana Guzmán'. Ominous, low synth music begins, underscoring the tense atmosphere as the Man in red t-shirt expresses personal apprehension, stating, 'Thankfully I live far away. If any of them got a hold of me in the future, I'd be in serious trouble.' He continues his tour, reiterating that these former enemy gangs now share the same cells. The scene shifts to barred cell doors, showing more tattooed inmates, some with light blue hair, standing in formation. The Man in red t-shirt gestures towards the cells, emphasizing the scale and the harsh reality that 'Their entire lives are confined within this cell. They can never leave. This is their toilet,' pointing out its location in the corner. Close-ups reveal individual inmates, including one with a somber expression, accompanied by Arabic subtitles translating to 'and later murdered her.' The segment concludes with a wide shot of the controlled prison environment, with the Man in red t-shirt continuing his commentary and tour amidst the vigilant masked prison guards.

The video opens with the Man in red t-shirt, identified as the narrator, speaking into a microphone in front of a barred cell at CECOT prison, explaining that inmates have been there for two years since the prison opened and all criminals were transferred there. He gestures toward Tattooed inmates behind bars, many shirtless and displaying extensive tattoos, including names like 'Santana Guzmán' and symbolic imagery. The narrator continues, explaining that Masked prison guards cover their faces out of fear that inmates might contact outsiders for revenge on their families. The scene transitions to a wide shot of a prison corridor as the Man in red t-shirt walks down the hall, flanked by Masked prison guards, passing Tattooed inmates sitting cross-legged on the floor, some with visible handcuffs and chains. He gestures toward the camera, then proceeds down the corridor, stating they are heading to the solitary confinement cells where any prisoner who misbehaves ends up. He is escorted by a Guard in black uniform toward a heavy metal door marked 'CECOT 5', which opens with a distinct sound to reveal a dark, bare Cell interior. The narrator describes it as having 'no light' and 'only a cement slab.' The Man in red t-shirt enters, holding a flashlight, and gestures to the sparse interior, emphasizing the minimal conditions, with the narrator adding that misbehaving prisoners are placed there for 15 days. He then exits and walks down another corridor, passing a cell with a yellow bucket and a sink. Arabic subtitles, translating to 'and later murdered her,' appear during a close-up of a somber inmate. The scene shifts to a dining area where a Worker in a hairnet pushes a Food distribution cart of stacked plastic containers, handing them through the bars to inmates. The narrator explains that inmates 'don't even leave their cells for meals' and 'food is delivered inside.' Close-ups show the Food container being distributed—containing 'beans and rice, no chicken, no meat'—into individual containers, with inmates reaching through the bars. The environment is stark, with cold lighting and a clinical atmosphere, underscored by faint, somber background music, emphasizing the institutional nature of the prison.

The minute opens in a prison corridor with a worker in a hairnet and gloves pushing a food distribution cart, handing meals of rice, beans, and bread to masked, tattooed inmates in white uniforms through bars, accompanied by faint clatter and a low hum. The scene then shifts to an aerial view of the sprawling CECOT prison complex at dusk, before cutting to a stark, white-walled cell. Here, the Man in red t-shirt, flanked by two silent Masked prison guards in riot gear, interviews a handcuffed inmate with a large chest tattoo. A male voiceover states, 'Later, I interviewed one of the gang members,' identifying the inmate as an 'MS-13 member.' The interviewer is heard asking, 'So, how many person he killed?' to which a voice responds, '30,' and the interviewer confirms, 'He's killed 30 people.' The interviewer then asks, 'So, can we see his tattoo?' and another voice prompts, 'Look,' as the inmate reveals more of his extensive chest tattoos, including a prominent 'MS' design. Arabic subtitles appear, translating the interviewer's questions like 'Do you regret what you did?' and 'How do you feel about your punishment?' The interviewer is heard asking, 'You regret what you did in the past?' and the inmate responds somberly in Spanish. A brief cut to a wider corridor showing other inmates being escorted by guards precedes a return to the interview, where the inmate continues to speak in Spanish, reflecting on his situation. The interview concludes with the inmate looking down, as an English voiceover summarizes his confession: 'He says he cries every day and regrets what he has done, but it's too late. He feels remorse.'

event_blocks.summary: Inside a dimly lit prison cell, a man in a red t-shirt questions an inmate in a white uniform, flanked by masked guards, about when he joined a gang, asking, "So when he start enter the gang, like how old he was?" Arabic subtitles translate the question, and the inmate replies in Spanish, "Como la de 11 años," translated as '11 years old.' The narrator states the inmate joined the gang at 11 and is now in CECOT, as reflective music begins. An aerial view shows the modern CECOT prison complex, and the man in red t-shirt walks through sunlit corridors, praising President Nayib Bukele's actions and noting his 2019 approval rating was around 50%. A montage displays a 2019 map of El Salvador with Bukele's portrait and '53.1% of the vote.' Bukele speaks from the Palacio Nacional balcony at night, declaring, "Today, El Salvador has broken all the records," to a cheering crowd, with Arabic subtitles and triumphant music. The map reappears showing 2024 election results with '64.65% of the vote,' though the narrator claims Bukele won by a landslide with 85%. Bukele is heard declaring El Salvador "the safest country in the entire Western Hemisphere" amidst falling confetti, with the sound of confetti audible, as the narrator explains the people finally felt safe. Children smile in a sunny park, symbolizing safety, and the narrator concludes the tour of CECOT prison, suggesting much can be learned from the president. The man in red t-shirt walks through the CECOT corridor towards a guard at a doorway, silhouetted by a golden sunset, accompanied by the sound of a door and a final musical flourish.

Arabic subtitles.description: On-screen text in Arabic, appearing during the close-up of a somber inmate, translating to 'Do you regret what you did?' and 'How do you feel about your punishment?', adding a narrative layer to the visual content and suggesting a backstory of violence or crime.

Map of El Salvador.description: A digital map highlighting El Salvador in Central America, with animated markers for gangs 'MS-13' and 'Barrio 18' appearing over the country, indicating gang territories.

Children in park.description: Three children, two girls and one boy, smiling at the camera in a sunny park with blooming trees and greenery in the background, symbolizing safety and normalcy.

Inmate in white uniform.description: A seated inmate in a white prison uniform with a shaved head, appearing somber and restrained with handcuffs and chains visible, who responds in Spanish, 'Como la de 11 años' (11 years old), to a question about joining a gang.

Man in red t-shirt.description: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, seen gesturing and asking a seated inmate, 'So when he start enter the gang, like how old he was?', seemingly conducting an interview or interrogation within the CECOT prison.

Sunset landscape.description: A wide, scenic view of rolling hills and mountains at sunset, visible through the open doorway of the CECOT prison, with golden light illuminating the horizon and contrasting with the prison's stark interior.

Masked prison guards.description: Uniformed officers in gray shirts and black balaclavas, standing along the corridor with batons, observing the inmates’ movements, maintaining a vigilant and controlled presence throughout the sequence.

Nayib Bukele.description: The President of El Salvador, shown in formal attire during a public address from the Palacio Nacional balcony, speaking in English to a cheering crowd, declaring 'Today, El Salvador has broken all the records' and El Salvador as 'the safest country in the entire Western Hemisphere'.

CECOT prison.description: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, signage reading 'ARMERÍA' and 'CECOT', featuring well-lit corridors where the Man in red t-shirt walks, and identified by a narrator as the location where the inmate is held and the subject of a 'tour'.

Palacio Nacional.description: A grand, illuminated neoclassical building with columns and a balcony, where Nayib Bukele delivers a speech to a crowd, with confetti falling as he declares El Salvador the safest country in the Western Hemisphere.

raw_segments: The minute opens inside a stark, dimly lit prison cell where a man in a red t-shirt faces a somber inmate in white prison garb, flanked by two masked guards. A narrator states, "but he must face justice," as the man in red t-shirt asks the inmate, "So when he start enter the gang, like how old he was?" Arabic subtitles translate the question, and the inmate responds in Spanish, "Como la de 11 años," which is translated on screen as '11 years old'. The narrator then emphasizes, "He joined the gang when he was just 11 years old, and now he is here in CECOT." Gentle, reflective background music begins as the scene shifts to an aerial view of the modern CECOT prison complex. The man in red t-shirt is then seen walking through its sunlit corridors, arms outstretched, passing a guard, while the narrator praises President Nayib Bukele's "bold and brilliant" actions, noting his 2019 approval rating was "around 50%." A political montage follows, highlighting a map of El Salvador in 2019 with Bukele's portrait and '53.1% of the vote'. Bukele is then shown speaking from the Palacio Nacional balcony at night, declaring in English, "Today, El Salvador has broken all the records," to a cheering crowd, with Arabic subtitles and triumphant music. The map reappears, showing 2024 election results with '64.65% of the vote', though the narrator claims Bukele "won by a landslide with 85% of the vote." Bukele is then heard declaring El Salvador "the safest country in the entire Western Hemisphere" amidst falling confetti, with the sound of confetti audible. The narrator explains, "Why? Because for the first time, the people felt safe." The video cuts to children smiling in a sunny park, symbolizing safety, as the narrator concludes, "This is the end of our tour of CECOT prison. What did you learn from this episode? There's a lot we can learn from this president." The scene returns to the man in red t-shirt walking through the CECOT prison corridor, approaching a guard at a doorway with a golden sunset behind him, accompanied by the sound of a door and a final musical flourish.

event_blocks.summary: The scene opens with an expansive aerial view of a rolling landscape at sunset, featuring a sky painted in deep blues and purples streaked with orange and pink near the horizon. Below, a vast expanse of green fields and scattered trees stretches toward distant mountains, appearing as dark silhouettes against the twilight sky. The gently undulating terrain, with patches of dense vegetation and open grasslands, is bathed in soft, golden light, casting long shadows and highlighting the land's textures. This tranquil, untouched natural atmosphere, devoid of human presence or structures, is underscored by a subtle, melancholic, and ethereal musical score, enhancing the peaceful isolation and natural beauty of the wide, scenic vista.

Sunset landscape.description: A wide, scenic view of rolling hills and mountains at sunset, with golden light illuminating the horizon and deep blue-purple clouds above, creating a tranquil and expansive natural vista, profoundly underscored by a subtle, melancholic, and ethereal musical score.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a serene and expansive aerial view of a rolling landscape at sunset, a stark contrast to previous sequences. The sky is painted in deep blues and purples, streaked with orange and pink near the horizon, suggesting the sun has recently set or is setting. Below, a vast expanse of green fields and scattered trees stretches toward distant mountains, which appear as dark silhouettes against the twilight sky. The gently undulating terrain, with patches of dense vegetation and open grasslands, is bathed in soft, golden light, casting long shadows and highlighting the textures of the land. This tranquil, untouched natural atmosphere, devoid of human figures, structures, or movement, is profoundly underscored by a subtle, melancholic, and ethereal musical score that plays throughout, enhancing the peaceful isolation and natural beauty of this wide, scenic vista.

global_entity_library.El Salvador cityscape: An aerial view of a densely built city with greenery and a prominent volcano in the background, likely San Salvador, shown during a drive through the region, accompanied by car sounds and indistinct chatter from inside the van.
global_entity_library.Tattooed inmates: Inmates in white uniforms, many with extensive tattoos on their chests, arms, and heads, including names like 'Santana Guzmán', dates, and symbolic imagery like skulls and women’s faces. They are seen sitting on the floor in a line, some lifting their shirts to reveal tattoos, with handcuffs and chains visible on their wrists and ankles. One specific inmate is identified by a voiceover as an 'MS-13 member' and is heard responding in Spanish to questions about his past actions and regrets, with an English voiceover summarizing his remorse.
global_entity_library.Soldiers in formation: Uniformed military personnel in helmets and tactical gear standing in rows during a public address by Bukele.
global_entity_library.Prison exterior at night: A dimly lit prison yard with high walls, barbed wire, and a watchtower, shown during a vehicle approach, reinforcing the high-security environment, as voiceovers discuss the state of emergency.
global_entity_library.Man in red t-shirt: A man with a beard wearing a red t-shirt with 'LES BELLAMINS B.S.P.H.C UNDERGROUND' printed on the back, seen gesturing and asking a seated inmate, 'So when he start enter the gang, like how old he was?', seemingly conducting an interview or interrogation within the CECOT prison.
global_entity_library.Masked prison guards: Uniformed officers in gray shirts and black balaclavas, standing along the corridor with batons, observing the inmates’ movements, maintaining a vigilant and controlled presence throughout the sequence.
global_entity_library.Nayib Bukele: The President of El Salvador, shown in formal attire during a public address from the Palacio Nacional balcony, speaking in English to a cheering crowd, declaring 'Today, El Salvador has broken all the records' and El Salvador as 'the safest country in the entire Western Hemisphere'.
global_entity_library.News montage: A compilation of news clips showing armed soldiers in tactical gear during a state of emergency, with on-screen text from NBC News Now stating 'El Salvador records deadliest day in 30 years', and Arabic subtitles translating the context, accompanied by male voiceovers explaining that "El Salvador is under state of emergency" and that "a state of emergency was declared."
global_entity_library.CECOT prison: A modern, high-security prison complex with white and blue walls, signage reading 'ARMERÍA' and 'CECOT', featuring well-lit corridors where the Man in red t-shirt walks, and identified by a narrator as the location where the inmate is held and the subject of a 'tour'.
global_entity_library.Camera operator: A person holding a professional camera with a stabilizer, filming the man in the red t-shirt as he goes through the security checkpoint inside the CECOT prison, visible in the background during the process.
global_entity_library.Security table: A metal table with a bottle of hand sanitizer and a phone scanner, where the man hands over his belongings for inspection by the guard.
global_entity_library.Metal detector: A large white and blue 'Intercept' metal detector with a red light strip, branded with 'Tek84 San Diego, CA USA', used to screen individuals entering the CECOT prison, which a narrator identifies as a 'high-tech device' for verifying both prisoners and staff.
global_entity_library.Signage: Spanish signs including 'ÁREA MÉDICA' above the medical area entrance and 'COCINA' on a door in the hallway, indicating facility functions and restricted zones, with the 'ÁREA MÉDICA' sign being passed as the guard explains medical services.
global_entity_library.Guard in black uniform: A prison guard in a black uniform with 'EL SALVADOR' on the sleeve, holding a clipboard and interacting with inmates, inspecting their tattoos and overseeing the processing procedure, speaking to the man in the red t-shirt.
global_entity_library.Map of El Salvador: A digital map highlighting El Salvador in Central America, with animated markers for gangs 'MS-13' and 'Barrio 18' appearing over the country, indicating gang territories.
global_entity_library.Watchtower and barbed wire: A concrete watchtower with a guard on the balcony, surrounded by multiple layers of barbed wire fencing, emphasizing the prison's security measures, which are seen from a car driving past with accompanying engine and road noise.
global_entity_library.Aerial night view of CECOT: An illuminated, grid-like aerial view of the CECOT prison complex at night, showcasing its extensive layout and high-security design with blue lighting.
global_entity_library.Weapons warehouse: A secure, well-lit room within the CECOT prison containing organized racks of rifles and other restraints, marked by signage as 'ARMERÍA', serving as the storage and distribution point for prison armaments, where the guard's voice explains the purpose and function of the various security tools.
global_entity_library.Masked prison guard: A uniformed officer in black attire, wearing a cap and sunglasses, who serves as the primary speaker and guide, explaining the purpose of the gym ('This gym is for the guards. It's all for the guards' well-being,' and coping 'from being there in front of those criminals all day'), guiding the man ('Come with me. Now we're about to enter the prisoners' area. This is the most important part of the video.'), emphasizing the high-security nature of the CECOT prison ('The world's most dangerous gang leaders are just a few steps away. Once they enter the Center of Confinement of Terrorism, they will never leave again. Once they pass through, through this gate, they never come out.'), and directing the man further into the facility ('We're now going to the cells. Come inside.'). He escorts the man through the CECOT prison.
